1.1 INTRODUCTION
Ensemble prediction is a method to predict the probability d istribution of forecast states, given a
probability distribution of random analysis error and mode l uncertainties. In ensemble systems, the
impact of analysis and model uncertainties on forecasts sta tes are simulated by starting the ensemble
of forecasts from different initial conditions, and comput ing each forecast evolution using schemes
designed to simulate model uncertainties. Since 1992, ensemble prediction systems have been part of the
operational forecasting suite at ECMWF (
Palmer et al., 1993) and NCEP (T oth and Kalnay, 1993, 1997). A
general description of the ﬁrst version of the ECMWF EPS can be found in Molteni et al. (1996), and more
recent versions of the operational ensemble forecasts are described by Leutbecher and Palmer (2008) and
Lang et al. (2021).
The implementations by ECMWF and NCEP in the early 90-s were f ollowed in 1995 by MSC Canada
(Houtekamer et al., 1996b,a) and in 1998 by BoM Australia. The reader is referred to Buizza et al. (2005)
for a comparison of the performance of the ECMWF , MSC and NCEP ensemble systems over the
northern hemisphere, and to Bourke et al. (2005) for a comparison of the performance of the ECMWF and
the BoM systems over the southern hemisphere. In the past dec ade, other meteorological centres have
started producing routinely ensemble-based probabilistic forecasts, see for example Park et al. (2008) for
comparison of the performance of eight global ensemble pred iction systems archived within the TIGGE
project. The reader is referred to Hagedorn et al. (2012) for a more recent comparison of the performance
of the ECMWF EPS and multi-model ensemble systems deﬁned by t he TIGGE ensembles.
The atmospheric model IFS is coupled from initial time to the ocean model NEMO (see Madec,
2008). The coupling uses the single executable framework descri bed by Mogensen et al. (2012b). The
ocean initial conditions come from the near-real-time comp onent of the operational ocean analysis
(NEMOV AR, see Mogensen et al., 2012a) and from the Ocean ReAnalysis System 5 (ORAS5, see
Zuo et al., 2017) for re-forecasts. Further details on the coupling are give n in 3.1.
The 50+1 medium-range non-linear integrations from day 0 to day 15 have a resolution of TCo1279. They
are initialised twice daily at 00 and 12 UTC. In addition, 100 +1 extended-range integrations from day
0 to day 46 are initialised at 00 UTC daily with a resolution of TCo319. In the vertical, all integrations
have 137 levels, with the model top at 1 Pa. The extended-rang e integrations start from t = 0 h since
27 June 2023. The two separate ensembles for medium-range an d extended-range supersede a variable-
resolution system, which was operational from 12 September 2006 (V AREPS, seeBuizza et al. (2007)) to
IFS Documentation – Cy48r1 3
Chapter 1: Methodology
27 June 2023. Earlier conﬁgurations of the extended-range/ monthly ensemble are described by Vitart
(2004) and Vitart et al. (2008). The separation into two separate ensembles has the follow ing advantages:
(i) it made it feasible to double the ensemble size for the ext ended-range forecasts from 50 to 100
perturbed members, (ii) it provides more ﬂexibility for fut ure revisions of the re-forecasts (see below),
and (iii) it permits users to generate dual-resolution ense mble forecast products based on 150 members
for the medium-range ( Leutbecher and Ben Bouall` egue, 2020; Baran et al., 2019).
On 11 March 2008, a new ensemble re-forecast suite was implem ented to estimate the model
climatological distribution (see Hagedorn, 2008 for a description of the system). Since 27 June 2023, there
are two reforecast suites for the medium-range and extended -range. Their resolutions are TCo1

catalog_entry.summary: This document provides details on the Ensemble Prediction System (EPS) of the Integrated Forecasting System (IFS), version CY48R1, including methodology and computational details.
